speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 6, March 18, 1997, interview Esther Gever. Continue please.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in [NON-ENGLISH] from 1944 to 1946. 1946, we were again loaded into a train, a cattle train by the way. And we were freed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that voluntary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not voluntary. We had nothing to do with it. We had known that the war had ended. But we weren't free to leave. We had to wait to be freed. We were taken-- took a couple of weeks. I don't remember how long the journey was. We arrived in a town called [NON-ENGLISH], which is the lower Silesia, [NON-ENGLISH]. And it once belonged to Germany, and it was taken back by Poland. In revenge for their aggression, it was returned to Poland. It's now Polish again. It was several times, I think, back and forth. It was a German town. It had all the characteristics, all the [NON-ENGLISH], the beautiful homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there till 19-- well, in 1948, I married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left--

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came there when?

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was May. I'm not sure. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not attend school. I worked. I took some courses. I took some Yiddish courses. I took some mathematical courses. I did not go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my father. My sister went to school. I had, between the house and a few courses here and there and a little work outside of it, I was quite busy all-- my father met a woman whom I, incidentally, did not approve of. And she made a little, mini Holocaust for us again. But he married her. I left. I lived on my own in the same building. I took an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16. I met my husband, and I was very lucky. He's a great gentleman and a very caring man.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Sol [INAUDIBLE] Gever. I have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948. I didn't have the children before. [LAUGHS] I got married in 1948. In 1949, I had my first child in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you immigrate to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I immigrated in 1949. I conceived in Poland. And she was born six months after that in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Otylia. We lived-- it was hard. Circumstances were tough. And It was [INAUDIBLE]. There was no work. My husband couldn't work at his profession. He worked by building--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: --buildings. He is a shoe designer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town did you live in in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], which was a-- [NON-ENGLISH] is the name of a sheikh that had 30 wives. There were 30 little buildings, rooms. And he deserted. He ran away when Israel became independent. And that's where we lived until we left for the United States, which was 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I skipped that. 1954, I had another daughter, Doreen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. 1954 in Israel. We came to the United States. My older daughter was 9. Doreen was 4 and 1/2. And they adapted. They like American girls. They finished school here. And I adapted very well to the United States. I love being here. It is a-- I love Israel. Israel is in my heart. I thought I would never leave Israel. But circumstances, sometimes, demanded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The climate was very hard on me. I had not felt well there. And there was an interference from my husband's American family that insisted we should come to the United States. My husband served in the army in Israel. He was in the Sinai Campaign for three weeks. I didn't know whether he survived-- is alive. There are little things that continue. Life continues. But we have-- right now, I spend a lot of time writing. I am very happy doing that. I am glad that they couldn't kill that thing in me. That instinct--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Olivia. And one is Jordana. Two girls. Lovely children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your experience influenced the way in which you raise children in some way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think that life experiences make us what we-- who we are and what we are. Of course, during the war, my aim was not to see-- not to absorb the ugly things, not to absorb anything cheap and dirty and cruel so that I could be a whole person again. And the life that I had till the age of 8, my treasure chest as I call it, gave me that strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you think it affected your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I might have been over protective. I don't know. I need a lot of love because it was missing so much in my life. And I give. I have such a boundless amount. All that I had to give to everybody, and I like warmth. I like to be with friends. I don't have casual relationships. I'm really very devoted and hope to have the same, someone who will reciprocate the same warmth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in search of it all the time. We need it. It is a part of the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be true to yourself. Fight for freedom and don't discriminate and don't let anyone discriminate. We have to see the evil before it grows into something so tremendous that it's too late because if we overlook it, it will eat us. It will eat up our children. It will engulf us. And therefore, sitting idle and saying, oh, it's nothing or it will never happen, that's what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: May we hear one of the poems that are your treasure chest?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This poem is called 'Kristallnacht.' 'November, the month when trees shed their last leaves, the month of Kristallnacht when childhood has ceased, the season for promise of long winter nights ahead and memories of shortened lives and tears to share my restless bed. 57 Novembers past since the gruesome Kristallnacht. Along with our martyrs, part of our souls remained behind among the broken glass. Family, friends, neighbors frozen in terror and fear. Incomprehensible evil roamed. Loud echoes of helpless screams, the world refused to hear. Black boots and long leather coats worn by soulless men, their sadistic eyes beaming with hate executing the devil's plan. Now, a grandmother twice, with heart full of good will, I wonder about my childhood and searching for it still. I wonder how it would have been to grow up under mother's loving care in a world of love and freedom and chance to earn equal fare. I wonder how many Novembers on this earth I have still left. There are so many stories to tell and endless tears to shed. Going back 57 Novembers, to a little girl of only 8, a long history of pain, loss, and survival. The victim's humanity has prevailed.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Indeed, it has--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is my grandfather, Shmuel [? Schefter ?] who didn't take pictures. This was done by my uncle when he didn't look. It was a sketch. And from that sketch, we have this photograph. This is my grandmother, Sarah [? Schefter ?] and my cousin [INAUDIBLE] from [NON-ENGLISH] My grandfather's picture was before. And that is his wife. This is my maternal grandmother from Vienna. And next to her is one of my cousins. They both perished in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are Bella Mandle and Yosef Mandle.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken during the time of Hitler's reign. It was-- I got it from someone that survived and gave me that picture. You can see it's already sad-- my grandmother is sad. This is Israel Moses and Ethel Schachter. These are my parents. This picture was taken approximately in 1929 or 1930. This is a picture of myself. I am eight years old here. And this is the picture that was supposed to be sent to England along with myself. And I refused to go. This is a picture of Israel Moses Schachter, my father. And he's a bachelor here. It was before he got married. That must have been in the '20s, 1920s. This is my father. The picture is taken in Israel in the 1980s with his great-granddaughter, Jordana. He's about 85 in this picture. This is a picture of my family, my daughter Doreen on the right. Next to her, is Olivia, my husband, Jordana, myself, and my son-in-law on the left. And that's a picture about four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Son-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Son-in-law is Ronnie [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, [INAUDIBLE]. And she lives in New Jersey. I don't see enough of her. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Gever, would you introduce your family to us please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on my left is my loving husband, Sol, my sister, Ruth, on my right, my granddaughter, Jordana, and my lovely daughter, Doreen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anyone like to say anything to or about Mrs. Gever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doreen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a small apartment in Rego Park, Queens, where-- in a lovely apartment filled with flowers and pictures of family and poetry that my mother writes and all the-- her history-- a short history of her life-- the past 20 years has been in this apartment. And I think it's amazing that one can look around here and know that, in contrast, what she has seen and what she has gone through-- both my parents-- and that contrast is what colors our life. And that she and my father and my aunt survived to love and be loved and to be able to tell their story to their grandchildren and to share this with the world, to document this and-- so it will never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually want to say a few words. My sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Louder, would you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was really my mother. When I was little, she took care of me and really took a big chunk of her life that she couldn't do the things that she needed to do as a child. And she took care of me. And thank God, probably I turned out to be better, too, because of that. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jordana?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say that I'm very proud of my grandmother, and I think that it's very important that she let people know of her experiences and let people know of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
